_comment: Target schema for the extraction pipeline. Each target = one sheet of the city collection workbook format. 'grid' is the canonical output grid (matches the collection template); 'keywords' drive the page locator; 'rules' are the transformation instructions given to the extractor (the rung-1 operations: merge/split bins).

Age_Serv_Num.description: Active member COUNTS by age (rows) x years of credited service (columns). Source: the AV's active-members-by-age-and-service exhibit (often titled 'Active Member Counts by Age and Service', 'Distribution of Active Members', or 'Schedule of Active Member Valuation Data').
Age_Serv_Num.unit: member count (people; may be fractional after splits)

grid.rows_meaning: age buckets: under 25, 25-29, 30-34, 35-39, 40-44, 45-49, 50-54, 55-59, 60-64, 65 and over (label = upper bound, '<25' and '70' are the open ends)

rules: Counts are ADDITIVE: when source bins are finer than a target bin (e.g. separate 'Under 20' and '20-24' rows for target '<25'), declare op 'sum' over those source rows.

When a source bin is coarser than the target bins (e.g. a single 'Over 30' service column covering targets '34' and '40'), declare op 'share_even' from that source column in EACH covered target column.
Empty source cells stay null (no members), not 0.
Transcribe the source table without its total row/column.

Age_Serv_Wage.description: Active member AVERAGE SALARY by age (rows) x years of credited service (columns). Source: the AV's average-salary-by-age-and-service exhibit (often adjacent to the counts table, titled 'Active Member Average Salary/Compensation by Age and Service').
rules: Averages are NOT additive: when source bins are finer than a target bin, declare op 'weighted_avg' over those source rows OR columns, and ALSO transcribe the corresponding member-COUNTS table as an additional source table (reference its index in 'weights_table') - the counts are the weights. This applies to column merges too (e.g. source 'Under 1' + '1 to 4' columns into target '4'): col op 'weighted_avg', never 'sum' or 'share_even'.
When a source bin is coarser than the target bins, declare op 'copy' from that source column into EACH covered target column (an average does not split).

SOME AVs PUBLISH NO AVERAGE-SALARY EXHIBIT, only TOTAL salary dollars by age and service (usually alongside or interleaved with the counts). Then transcribe BOTH tables (totals + counts) and declare derive = {"op": "ratio", "numerator_table": <totals>, "denominator_table": <counts>} with ADDITIVE maps only (sum/copy, no weighted_avg): code computes average = total/count after aggregation.
Suppressed cells (e.g. '*' for fewer than N members) are transcribed as the string '*'.
Transcribe the source table without its total row/column and without overall-average rows.

Ret_Rate.description: Probability of retirement by service-year bucket (rows) x single age (columns). Source: the AV's demographic-assumptions section ('Retirement rates', 'Probability of Retirement'), usually printed as age rows x service-bucket columns - the reverse of the target orientation.

grid.cols_meaning: single attained ages 50 through 69; '70' = age 70 and over

rules: The source table is usually printed with age ROWS and service COLUMNS - the reverse of the target: transcribe it as printed and set transpose=true.

Rates are INTENSIVE (probabilities): never sum them. A target bin lying inside one source bin copies its rate; a target bin spanning several source bins uses op 'overlap_weighted' with the source bins' numeric spans declared in 'source_spans' - code blends proportionally by years.
Declare every source bin's span from its printed label ('<15' -> [null, 14], '15-24' -> [15, 24], '>31' -> [32, null]); if a boundary is ambiguous, state your reading in notes.
Source tables print percentages: transcribe the numbers as printed and set values_unit='percent'; code converts to decimals.
Use overlap_weighted with the printed age-bin spans for target age columns too when the source groups ages (e.g. '50-51', '66-69'); single-age targets inside a bin then copy its rate.
